mn57:1.2: At one time, the *Bhagavā* was dwelling among the Koliyans, at a town of the Koliyans named Haliddavasana.
mn57:10.1: And what, Puṇṇa, is black-and-white *kamma*, with black-and-white result?
mn57:10.10: This is called black-and-white *kamma*, with black-and-white result.
mn57:10.2: Here, Puṇṇa, someone constructs both an afflictive and a non-afflictive bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], constructs both an afflictive and a non-afflictive verbal *saṅkhāra* [*vacīsaṅkhāraṁ*], constructs both an afflictive and a non-afflictive mental *saṅkhāra* [*manosaṅkhāraṁ*].
mn57:10.3: Having constructed both an afflictive and a non-afflictive bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], having constructed both an afflictive and a non-afflictive verbal *saṅkhāra* [*vacīsaṅkhāraṁ*], having constructed both an afflictive and a non-afflictive mental *saṅkhāra* [*manosaṅkhāraṁ*], he is reborn in a world that is both afflictive and non-afflictive.
mn57:10.4: When he has been reborn in that world that is both afflictive and non-afflictive, both afflictive and non-afflictive contacts touch him.
mn57:10.5: Touched by both afflictive and non-afflictive contacts, he feels both afflictive and non-afflictive *vedanā*, mixed with *sukha* and *dukkha* [*vokiṇṇasukhadukkhaṁ*], just like humans, and some gods, and some beings in states of loss.
mn57:10.6: So, Puṇṇa, there is rebirth of a being according to what it has become;
mn57:10.7: what one does is what one is reborn into.
mn57:10.9: This is how I say, Puṇṇa, ‘beings are heirs to *kamma* [*kammadāyādā*].’
mn57:11.1: And what, Puṇṇa, is *kamma* that is neither black nor white, with neither-black-nor-white result, which leads to the exhaustion of *kamma* [*kammakkhayāya*]?
mn57:11.2: Therein, Puṇṇa, whatever intention there is for abandoning that black *kamma* with black result, whatever intention there is for abandoning that white *kamma* with white result, whatever intention there is for abandoning that black-and-white *kamma* with black-and-white result—
mn57:11.3: this is called *kamma* that is neither black nor white, with neither-black-nor-white result, which leads to the exhaustion of *kamma* [*kammakkhayāya*].
mn57:11.4: These, Puṇṇa, are the four *kammas* that have been made known by me after directly knowing and realizing them for myself.”
mn57:12.1: When this was said, Puṇṇa the Koliyan son, the cow-duty practitioner, said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn57:12.4: Let the *Bhagavā* remember me as a lay follower who from today has gone for refuge for life.”
mn57:13.1: But the naked ascetic Seniya, the dog-duty practitioner, said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn57:13.3: Just as if, *bhante*, … it has been made clear.
mn57:13.4: I go for refuge to the *Bhagavā*, and to the *dhamma*, and to the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghañca*].
mn57:13.5: May I receive the going forth in the presence of the *Bhagavā*; may I receive full ordination.”
mn57:14.1: “One, Seniya, who previously belonged to another sect, if he wishes for the going forth in this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*], wishes for full ordination, lives on probation for four months. After four months have passed, the *bhikkhus*, with confident minds [*āraddhacittā*], let him go forth and give him full ordination for the state of a *bhikkhu* [*bhikkhubhāvāya*].
mn57:14.2: But in this matter I recognize individual differences.”
mn57:14.3: “If, *bhante*, those who previously belonged to another sect, wishing for the going forth in this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*], wishing for full ordination, live on probation for four months, and after four months have passed the *bhikkhus*, with confident minds [*āraddhacittā*], let them go forth and give them full ordination for the state of a *bhikkhu* [*bhikkhubhāvāya*], then I will live on probation for four years. After four years have passed, may the *bhikkhus*, with confident minds [*āraddhacittā*], let me go forth and give me full ordination for the state of a *bhikkhu* [*bhikkhubhāvāya*].”
mn57:15.1: The naked ascetic Seniya, the dog-duty practitioner, received the going forth in the presence of the *Bhagavā*; he received full ordination.
mn57:15.2: And not long after his full ordination, the Venerable Seniya, dwelling alone, withdrawn, diligent, ardent, and resolute, soon realized for himself with direct knowledge, in this very life, that unsurpassed consummation of the holy life for the sake of which clansmen rightly go forth from home to homelessness, and having attained it, he dwelt in it.
mn57:15.3: He directly knew: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more for this state of being.’
mn57:15.4: And the Venerable Seniya became one of the *arahants*.
mn57:15.5: The Dog-Duty Practitioner, the seventh, is finished.
mn57:2.1: Then Puṇṇa, the Koliyan son, the cow-duty practitioner, and the naked ascetic Seniya, the dog-duty practitioner, approached the *Bhagavā*. Having approached, Puṇṇa the Koliyan son, the cow-duty practitioner, paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. But the naked ascetic Seniya, the dog-duty practitioner, exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā*. After exchanging courteous and cordial conversation, he crouched down like a dog and sat down to one side.
mn57:2.10: For a long time he has undertaken that dog observance fully and thoroughly.
mn57:2.11: What is his destination, what is his future course?”
mn57:2.13: Enough, Puṇṇa, let that be; do not ask me that.
mn57:2.14: But still, I will explain it to you.
mn57:2.2: While sitting to one side, Puṇṇa the Koliyan son, the cow-duty practitioner, said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn57:2.3: “This naked ascetic Seniya, the dog-duty practitioner, *bhante*, is one who does difficult practices; he eats food placed on the ground.
mn57:2.4: For a long time he has undertaken that dog observance fully and thoroughly.
mn57:2.5: What is his destination, what is his future course?”
mn57:2.6: “Enough, Puṇṇa, let that be; do not ask me that.”
mn57:2.7: A second time too Puṇṇa the Koliyan son, the cow-duty practitioner, …
mn57:2.8: A third time too Puṇṇa the Koliyan son, the cow-duty practitioner, said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn57:2.9: “This naked ascetic Seniya, the dog-duty practitioner, *bhante*, is one who does difficult practices; he eats food placed on the ground.
mn57:3.1: Here, Puṇṇa, someone develops a dog observance fully and unbroken, develops dog conduct fully and unbroken, develops a dog’s mind [*kukkuracittaṁ*] fully and unbroken, develops dog behavior fully and unbroken.
mn57:3.2: Having developed a dog observance fully and unbroken, having developed dog conduct fully and unbroken, having developed a dog’s mind [*kukkuracittaṁ*] fully and unbroken, having developed dog behavior fully and unbroken, with the breakup of the body, after death, he is reborn in companionship with dogs.
mn57:3.3: But if he has such a view: ‘By this ethical conduct, or observance, or austerity, or holy life, I will become a god or one among the gods,’ that is his wrong view.
mn57:3.4: For one with wrong view, Puṇṇa, I speak of one of two destinations: hell or animal birth.
mn57:3.5: So, Puṇṇa, when successful, dog observance leads to companionship with dogs; when unsuccessful, to hell.”
mn57:4.1: When this was said, the naked ascetic Seniya, the dog-duty practitioner, cried and shed tears.
mn57:4.10: “Enough, Seniya, let that be; do not ask me that.”
mn57:4.11: A second time too the naked ascetic Seniya …
mn57:4.12: A third time too the naked ascetic Seniya, the dog-duty practitioner, said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn57:4.13: “This is Puṇṇa, the Koliyan son, the cow-duty practitioner, *bhante*.
mn57:4.14: For a long time he has undertaken that cow observance fully and thoroughly.
mn57:4.15: What is his destination, what is his future course?”
mn57:4.17: Enough, Seniya, let that be; do not ask me that.
mn57:4.18: But still, I will explain it to you.
mn57:4.2: Then the *Bhagavā* said this to Puṇṇa the Koliyan son, the cow-duty practitioner:
mn57:4.3: “This is what I did not want from you, Puṇṇa.
mn57:4.4: Enough, Puṇṇa, let that be; do not ask me that.”
mn57:4.5: “It is not because the *Bhagavā* said this to me that I am crying, *bhante*;
mn57:4.6: rather, *bhante*, this dog observance of mine has long been fully and thoroughly undertaken.
mn57:4.7: This is Puṇṇa, the Koliyan son, the cow-duty practitioner, *bhante*.
mn57:4.8: For a long time he has undertaken that cow observance fully and thoroughly.
mn57:4.9: What is his destination, what is his future course?”
mn57:5.1: Here, Seniya, someone develops a cow observance fully and unbroken, develops cow conduct fully and unbroken, develops a cow’s mind [*gocittaṁ*] fully and unbroken, develops cow behavior fully and unbroken.
mn57:5.2: Having developed a cow observance fully and unbroken, having developed cow conduct fully and unbroken, having developed a cow’s mind [*gocittaṁ*] fully and unbroken, having developed cow behavior fully and unbroken, with the breakup of the body, after death, he is reborn in companionship with cattle.
mn57:5.3: But if he has such a view: ‘By this ethical conduct, or observance, or austerity, or holy life, I will become a god or one among the gods,’ that is his wrong view.
mn57:5.4: For one with wrong view, Seniya, I speak of one of two destinations: hell or animal birth.
mn57:5.5: So, Seniya, when successful, cow observance leads to companionship with cattle; when unsuccessful, to hell.”
mn57:6.1: When this was said, Puṇṇa the Koliyan son, the cow-duty practitioner, cried and shed tears.
mn57:6.10: “Yes, *bhante*,” Puṇṇa the Koliyan son, the cow-duty practitioner, replied to the *Bhagavā*.
mn57:6.2: Then the *Bhagavā* said this to the naked ascetic Seniya, the dog-duty practitioner:
mn57:6.3: “This is what I did not want from you, Seniya.
mn57:6.4: Enough, Seniya, let that be; do not ask me that.”
mn57:6.5: “It is not because the *Bhagavā* said this to me that I am crying, *bhante*;
mn57:6.6: rather, *bhante*, this cow observance of mine has long been fully and thoroughly undertaken.
mn57:6.7: I am so confident in the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*;
mn57:6.8: the *Bhagavā* is able to teach the *dhamma* in such a way that I may abandon this cow observance, and that this naked ascetic Seniya, the dog-duty practitioner, may abandon that dog observance.”
mn57:6.9: “Then, Puṇṇa, listen and apply appropriate attention [*yoniso manasikāra*] well. I will speak.”
mn57:7.1: “These four *kammas*, Puṇṇa, have been made known by me after directly knowing and realizing them for myself.
mn57:7.3: There is, Puṇṇa, black *kamma*, with black result;
mn57:7.4: there is, Puṇṇa, white *kamma*, with white result;
mn57:7.5: there is, Puṇṇa, black-and-white *kamma*, with black-and-white result;
mn57:7.6: there is, Puṇṇa, *kamma* that is neither black nor white, with neither-black-nor-white result, which leads to the exhaustion of *kamma* [*kammakkhayāya*].
mn57:8.1: And what, Puṇṇa, is black *kamma*, with black result?
mn57:8.10: This is called black *kamma*, with black result.
mn57:8.2: Here, Puṇṇa, someone constructs an afflictive bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], constructs an afflictive verbal *saṅkhāra* [*vacīsaṅkhāraṁ*], constructs an afflictive mental *saṅkhāra* [*manosaṅkhāraṁ*].
mn57:8.3: Having constructed an afflictive bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], having constructed an afflictive verbal *saṅkhāra* [*vacīsaṅkhāraṁ*], having constructed an afflictive mental *saṅkhāra* [*manosaṅkhāraṁ*], he is reborn in an afflictive world.
mn57:8.4: When he has been reborn in that afflictive world, afflictive contacts touch him.
mn57:8.5: Touched by afflictive contacts, he feels afflictive *vedanā*, nothing but *dukkha* [*ekantadukkhaṁ*], just like beings in hell.
mn57:8.6: So, Puṇṇa, there is rebirth of a being according to what it has become;
mn57:8.7: what one does is what one is reborn into,
mn57:8.8: and once reborn, contacts touch one.
mn57:8.9: This is how I say, Puṇṇa, ‘beings are heirs to *kamma* [*kammadāyādā*].’
mn57:9.1: And what, Puṇṇa, is white *kamma*, with white result?
mn57:9.10: This is called white *kamma*, with white result.
mn57:9.2: Here, Puṇṇa, someone constructs a non-afflictive bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], constructs a non-afflictive verbal *saṅkhāra* [*vacīsaṅkhāraṁ*], constructs a non-afflictive mental *saṅkhāra* [*manosaṅkhāraṁ*].
mn57:9.3: Having constructed a non-afflictive bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], having constructed a non-afflictive verbal *saṅkhāra* [*vacīsaṅkhāraṁ*], having constructed a non-afflictive mental *saṅkhāra* [*manosaṅkhāraṁ*], he is reborn in a non-afflictive world.
mn57:9.4: When he has been reborn in that non-afflictive world, non-afflictive contacts touch him.
mn57:9.5: Touched by non-afflictive contacts, he feels non-afflictive *vedanā*, nothing but *sukha* [*ekantasukhaṁ*], just like the Subhakiṇha gods.
mn57:9.6: So, Puṇṇa, there is rebirth of a being according to what it has become;
mn57:9.7: what one does is what one is reborn into,
mn57:9.8: and once reborn, contacts touch one.
mn57:9.9: This is how I say, Puṇṇa, ‘beings are heirs to *kamma* [*kammadāyādā*].’
